title: Hubbard Hall — stakeholder analysis
text:
1 Hubbard Hall stakeholder analysis
This book explores Hubbard Hall’s stakeholders — donors, patrons, and class-takers. All charts are aggregates; no individual is identified. Data sources, definitions, and methods are in the Methodology appendix.
Headline findings


Code
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
from hh.io import read_parquet
from hh.viz import style, CATEGORICAL, BLUE, SEQUENTIAL_BLUE, INK, MUTED

households = read_parquet("processed", "households_summary.parquet")
regs = read_parquet("processed", "registrations_enriched.parquet")

# headline figures (computed, not hardcoded)
_order = ["[0, 1)", "[1, 5)", "[5, 10)", "[10, 20)", "[20, ∞)"]
_d = (
    households[households["is_donor"]]
    .groupby("distance_band", observed=True)["total_donations"]
    .sum()
    .reindex(_order)
    .fillna(0)
)
_c = _d.cumsum() / _d.sum()
within5, within10, within20 = (
    f"{_c.loc[b]:.0%}" for b in ("[1, 5)", "[5, 10)", "[10, 20)")
)
n_hh = f"{len(households):,}"
n_donors = f"{int(households['is_donor'].sum()):,}"
n_patrons = f"{int(households['is_patron'].sum()):,}"
n_class = f"{int(households['is_class_taker'].sum()):,}"
n_donor_class = f"{int((households['is_donor'] & households['is_class_taker']).sum()):,}"
n_all3 = f"{int((households['is_donor'] & households['is_patron'] & households['is_class_taker']).sum()):,}"

from hh.analytics.productions import is_excluded, count_succeeded_attendees

_near = ["[0, 1)", "[1, 5)", "[5, 10)"]
_cl = regs[regs["event_majorcat"] == "class"].dropna(subset=["distance_band"])
class_w10 = f"{_cl['distance_band'].isin(_near).mean():.0%}"
_th = regs[regs["event_minorcat"] == "theater"].dropna(subset=["distance_band"])
theater_w10 = f"{_th['distance_band'].isin(_near).mean():.0%}"
_perf = regs[(regs["event_majorcat"] == "performance") & ~regs["event_name"].map(is_excluded)].copy()
_perf["attendees"] = _perf["tickets"].map(count_succeeded_attendees)
_don = _perf.merge(households[["id", "is_donor"]], on="id", how="left")
donor_att_share = f"{_don.loc[_don['is_donor'].fillna(False), 'attendees'].sum() / _don['attendees'].sum():.0%}"



Donors are local. About 54% of donated dollars come from within 10 miles of Hubbard Hall (41% within 5 miles; 77% within 20 miles).
Theater draws on Saturday; music on Sunday. But within a theater run that plays Fri/Sat/Sun, Saturday leads only because it usually has two shows — per performance the three days are essentially tied, and Saturday matinees are the weekend’s weakest slot. The ranking is shifting: since 2021, Sunday has been the best-attended day per performance in nearly every full-weekend run. Details are in When people come to Hubbard Hall.
Classes are local; performances are regional. 76% of class registrations come from within 10 miles, versus 54% for theater — and Sunday draws the farthest-traveling theater audience. See How far do people travel to Hubbard Hall?.
Strong overlap: of 4,598 households, 1,298 donate, 2,745 attend performances, and 1,819 take classes; 629 both donate and take classes, 418 do all three.
Donor households fill most seats: they account for 68% of all performance attendance. See What performances do donors attend?.

title: 2  Where do donors live?
text:
2.1 Has donor geography changed over time?
How far do Hubbard Hall’s donors live from the hall? Distances are straight-line (haversine) from the venue (25 E Main St, Cambridge NY) to each donor household, bucketed into half-open mile bands. Donors whose only address is a PO box are placed at their ZIP’s centroid and flagged “approximate” (excluded from the tight 1-mile count so it stays honest).
The tightest band includes some approximate (ZIP-centroid) placements — its centroid share, to read the 1-mile count in context:
The same dollars, split into roughly five-year periods and shown as shares within each period, so changes in the mix are visible regardless of overall growth. (This uses gift-level dates, unlike the lifetime household totals above.)
Code
from hh.clean.accounts import clean_accounts
from hh.clean.donations import clean_donations
from hh.analytics.donors import succeeded_individual_gifts

PERIODS = ["2013–2015", "2016–2020", "2021–2026"]
BANDS = ["[0, 1)", "[1, 5)", "[5, 10)", "[10, 20)", "[20, ∞)"]
BAND_COLORS = dict(zip(BANDS, ["#b7d3f6", "#6da7ec", "#3987e5", "#184f95", "#0d366b"]))

accounts_geo = read_parquet("processed", "accounts_geocoded.parquet")
gifts = succeeded_individual_gifts(clean_donations(accounts=clean_accounts()))
gifts["distance_band"] = gifts["account_id"].map(
    accounts_geo.set_index("account_id")["distance_band"]
)
gifts["period"] = pd.cut(
    gifts["donation_date"].dt.year, bins=[2010, 2015, 2020, 2026], labels=PERIODS
)
gd = (
    gifts.dropna(subset=["distance_band", "period"])
    .groupby(["period", "distance_band"], observed=True)["donation_amount"]
    .sum()
    .reset_index()
)
gd["share"] = gd.groupby("period", observed=True)["donation_amount"].transform(
    lambda s: s / s.sum()
)
w10 = {
    p: f"{gd[(gd['period'] == p) & (gd['distance_band'].isin(BANDS[:3]))]['share'].sum():.0%}"
    for p in PERIODS
}
Code
fig = px.bar(
    gd, x="period", y="share", color="distance_band",
    color_discrete_map=BAND_COLORS, category_orders={"distance_band": BANDS},
)
fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="Period", yaxis_title="Share of donated $", legend_title="Distance band"
)
fig.update_yaxes(tickformat=".0%")
style(fig).show()




                            
                                            


Figure 2.2: Donor-dollar share by distance band, by period
The within-10-mile share of donated dollars was 54% in 2013–2015, 54% in 2016–2020, and 51% in 2021–2026.

title: 3  When do people come to Hubbard Hall?
text:
3.1 Within a theater run: Friday, Saturday, or Sunday?
When the same production plays Friday, Saturday, and Sunday, which day do people pick? This chapter leads with that within-run view — the cleanest read on audience day preference, since it holds the show constant — then asks how the answer has changed over time, whether opera and music runs behave the same way, and closes with the coarser all-events view. How runs are reconstructed from Neon events, what is excluded, and how headcount is counted are in the Methodology appendix.
Only theater production runs that played all three of Friday, Saturday, and Sunday — mostly mainstage shows running one to four weekends. Attendance is true headcount, not registrations.
Code
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
from hh.io import read_parquet
from hh.viz import style, BLUE, CATEGORICAL
from hh.analytics.timing import WEEKDAY_ORDER
from hh.analytics.productions import production_performances, full_weekend_runs, time_slot

regs = read_parquet("processed", "registrations_enriched.parquet")
perf = production_performances(regs, minorcats=("theater",))
fw = full_weekend_runs(perf)

day_order = [d for d in WEEKDAY_ORDER if d in set(fw["weekday"])]
day = (
    fw.groupby("weekday")
    .agg(performances=("swept_event_id", "count"), attendees=("attendees", "sum"))
    .reindex(day_order)
)
day["attendees per performance"] = day["attendees"] / day["performances"]
day["share of attendees"] = day["attendees"] / day["attendees"].sum()

n_runs = fw["production_run"].nunique()
yr_span = f"{fw['starts_on'].min():%Y}–{fw['starts_on'].max():%Y}"
sat_share = f"{day.loc['Saturday', 'share of attendees']:.0%}"
per_perf = {
    d: f"{day.loc[d, 'attendees per performance']:.0f}"
    for d in ("Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday")
}
Across the 34 qualifying production runs (2013–2026), 296 performances drew 10,704 attendees. Any weekday rows below Friday–Sunday are the rare midweek performances inside a qualifying run (Winter Carnival 2015 played two Thursdays):
Code
day.style.format(
    {
        "performances": "{:,}",
        "attendees": "{:,}",
        "attendees per performance": "{:.1f}",
        "share of attendees": "{:.1%}",
    }
)

Saturday brings in the most people (42% of attendees) — but only because most runs play Saturday twice (matinee + evening). Per performance the three days are essentially tied: Friday 37, Saturday 35, and Sunday 37 attendees per show.
How each production splits across the three days (runs with fewer than 30 Fri–Sun attendees are left off the chart but appear in the table below):
Code
wk = fw[fw["weekday"].isin(["Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday"])]
run_day = wk.groupby(["production_run", "weekday"], as_index=False)["attendees"].sum()
run_day["share"] = run_day["attendees"] / run_day.groupby("production_run")[
    "attendees"
].transform("sum")
order = fw.groupby("production_run")["starts_on"].min().sort_values().index.tolist()
run_totals = wk.groupby("production_run")["attendees"].sum()
charted = [r for r in order if run_totals[r] >= 30]
DAY_COLORS = {"Friday": CATEGORICAL[0], "Saturday": CATEGORICAL[1], "Sunday": CATEGORICAL[2]}

fig = px.bar(
    run_day[run_day["production_run"].isin(charted)],
    x="share",
    y="production_run",
    color="weekday",
    orientation="h",
    category_orders={
        "production_run": charted[::-1],
        "weekday": ["Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday"],
    },
    color_discrete_map=DAY_COLORS,
    custom_data=["attendees"],
)
fig.update_traces(
    hovertemplate="%{x:.0%} — %{customdata[0]:,} attendees<extra>%{fullData.name}</extra>"
)
fig.update_layout(
    height=740,
    xaxis_tickformat=".0%",
    xaxis_title="Share of run's Fri–Sun attendees",
    yaxis_title="",
)
style(fig).show()




                            
                                            


Figure 3.1: Share of each run’s Fri–Sun attendees by day — one bar per production run, oldest at top
The same runs in detail — attendees by day, with each run’s dates and performance count:
Code
by_day = (
    fw.pivot_table(
        index="production_run",
        columns="weekday",
        values="attendees",
        aggfunc="sum",
        fill_value=0,
    )
    .reindex(columns=day_order)
)
by_day["Total"] = by_day.sum(axis=1)
meta = fw.groupby("production_run").agg(
    first=("starts_on", "min"), last=("starts_on", "max"), performances=("swept_event_id", "count")
)
detail = meta.join(by_day).reindex(order)
detail["first"] = detail["first"].dt.strftime("%b %d, %Y")
detail["last"] = detail["last"].dt.strftime("%b %d, %Y")
detail.index.name = None
detail = detail.rename(columns={"first": "First", "last": "Last", "performances": "Perfs"})
detail.style.format("{:,}", subset=[c for c in detail.columns if c not in ("First", "Last")])

Saturday’s totals hide one more split: most runs play a 2 pm matinee and an evening show that day. Splitting Saturday performances by time slot:
Code
sat = fw[fw["weekday"] == "Saturday"].assign(slot=lambda d: d["event_name"].map(time_slot))
slot = (
    sat.groupby("slot")
    .agg(performances=("swept_event_id", "count"), attendees=("attendees", "sum"))
    .reindex(["matinee", "evening"])
)
slot["attendees per performance"] = slot["attendees"] / slot["performances"]
mat_rate = f"{slot.loc['matinee', 'attendees per performance']:.0f}"
eve_rate = f"{slot.loc['evening', 'attendees per performance']:.0f}"
slot.style.format(
    {"performances": "{:,}", "attendees": "{:,}", "attendees per performance": "{:.1f}"}
)







 
performances

slot
 
 
 




matinee

Saturday evenings draw 39 attendees per show versus 29 for Saturday matinees — the weakest slot of the weekend. Sunday’s single 2 pm show (37 per show) draws as well as any slot, so the matinee habit is a Sunday habit, not a Saturday one.
crumbs: 3  When do people come to Hubbard Hall?

title: 3  When do people come to Hubbard Hall?
section: 3.2 Has the best day changed over time?
text:
3.2 Has the best day changed over time?
The numbers above pool 2013–2026, but the day ranking has not been stable. Still holding the product constant (full-weekend theater runs, attendees per performance so days with two shows don’t get double credit), here is the same question across roughly five-year periods.


Code
PERIODS = ["2013–2015", "2016–2020", "2021–2026"]

def period(year):
    return pd.cut(year, bins=[2010, 2015, 2020, 2026], labels=PERIODS)

run_rate = (
    wk.groupby(["production_run", "weekday"], as_index=False)
    .agg(performances=("attendees", "count"), attendees=("attendees", "sum"))
)
run_rate = run_rate[run_rate["production_run"].isin(charted)].copy()
run_rate["rate"] = run_rate["attendees"] / run_rate["performances"]
run_rate["run_start"] = run_rate["production_run"].map(
    wk.groupby("production_run")["starts_on"].min()
)

recent = run_rate[run_rate["run_start"] >= "2021-01-01"]
best_day = recent.loc[recent.groupby("production_run")["rate"].idxmax(), "weekday"]
n_recent = recent["production_run"].nunique()
n_recent_sun = int((best_day == "Sunday").sum())

wk_periods = wk.assign(period=period(wk["starts_on"].dt.year))
pd_rate = (
    wk_periods.groupby(["period", "weekday"], observed=True)
    .agg(performances=("attendees", "count"), attendees=("attendees", "sum"))
    .reset_index()
)
pd_rate["rate"] = pd_rate["attendees"] / pd_rate["performances"]
rate_of = lambda p, d: pd_rate.set_index(["period", "weekday"])["rate"].loc[(p, d)]


One dot per run and day — each run’s attendees per performance on Fridays, Saturdays, and Sundays:


Code
fig = px.scatter(
    run_rate.sort_values("run_start"),
    x="run_start",
    y="rate",
    color="weekday",
    color_discrete_map=DAY_COLORS,
    category_orders={"weekday": ["Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday"]},
    hover_name="production_run",
    custom_data=["performances", "attendees"],
)
fig.update_traces(
    hovertemplate=(
        "<b>%{hovertext}</b><br>%{y:.0f} attendees/perf "
        "(%{customdata[1]:,} over %{customdata[0]} shows)<extra>%{fullData.name}</extra>"
    )
)
for d in ("Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday"):  # 5-run rolling average as a guide line
    s = run_rate[run_rate["weekday"] == d].sort_values("run_start")
    fig.add_scatter(
        x=s["run_start"],
        y=s["rate"].rolling(5, center=True, min_periods=3).mean(),
        mode="lines",
        line=dict(color=DAY_COLORS[d], width=1.5),
        opacity=0.6,
        showlegend=False,
        hoverinfo="skip",
    )
fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="Run opening date", yaxis_title="Attendees per performance", legend_title=""
)
style(fig).show()




                            
                                            


Figure 3.2: Attendees per performance by day, one point per full-weekend theater run




Aggregated to periods:


Code
fig = px.bar(
    pd_rate,
    x="period",
    y="rate",
    color="weekday",
    barmode="group",
    color_discrete_map=DAY_COLORS,
    category_orders={"period": PERIODS, "weekday": ["Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday"]},
    custom_data=["performances"],
)
fig.update_traces(
    hovertemplate="%{y:.1f} attendees/perf (%{customdata[0]} shows)<extra>%{fullData.name}</extra>"
)
fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="Period", yaxis_title="Attendees per performance", legend_title=""
)
style(fig).show()




                            
                                            


Figure 3.3: Attendees per performance by day and period, full-weekend theater runs only




Per-performance attendance has roughly doubled across the periods, and the day ranking has flipped. In 2013–2015 Friday was the strongest day (27 per show vs 24 on Sunday); in 2016–2020 the days were about even; since 2021 Sunday is clearly strongest — 66 per show vs about 50 on Friday and Saturday. Sunday has been the best-attended day in 9 of the 9 full-weekend runs since 2021.
Recent-period caveats: 2021–2026 rests on 9 runs (52 performances), and recent runs rarely schedule Saturday matinees, which used to pull Saturday’s average down — yet Sunday leads anyway.
crumbs: 3  When do people come to Hubbard Hall?

title: 3  When do people come to Hubbard Hall?
section: 3.3 Do opera and music runs behave the same way?
text:
3.3 Do opera and music runs behave the same way?
Music and opera get the same treatment: map event names to productions, keep runs that played all of Friday, Saturday, and Sunday. Almost all music events are one-off concerts (Music from Salem plays single afternoons, cabarets play one or two nights), so nearly everything that qualifies is opera — the summer festival stagings of 2013–2017, which also played real midweek (Wednesday/Thursday) performances — plus one 2015 musical folktale with a theater-style four-weekend run. No over-time comparison is possible: the qualifying runs all fall in 2013–2017.


Code
mo_fw = full_weekend_runs(production_performances(regs, minorcats=("music", "opera")))

mo_day_order = [d for d in WEEKDAY_ORDER if d in set(mo_fw["weekday"])]
mo_day = (
    mo_fw.groupby("weekday")
    .agg(performances=("swept_event_id", "count"), attendees=("attendees", "sum"))
    .reindex(mo_day_order)
)
mo_day["attendees per performance"] = mo_day["attendees"] / mo_day["performances"]
mo_day["share of attendees"] = mo_day["attendees"] / mo_day["attendees"].sum()
mo_rate = {d: f"{mo_day.loc[d, 'attendees per performance']:.0f}" for d in mo_day_order}
n_mo_runs = mo_fw["production_run"].nunique()


The 9 qualifying runs, chronologically:


Code
mo_by_day = mo_fw.pivot_table(
    index="production_run",
    columns="weekday",
    values="attendees",
    aggfunc="sum",
    fill_value=0,
).reindex(columns=mo_day_order)
mo_by_day["Total"] = mo_by_day.sum(axis=1)
mo_meta = mo_fw.groupby("production_run").agg(
    first=("starts_on", "min"), last=("starts_on", "max"), performances=("swept_event_id", "count")
)
mo_order = mo_meta["first"].sort_values().index.tolist()
mo_detail = mo_meta.join(mo_by_day).reindex(mo_order)
mo_detail["first"] = mo_detail["first"].dt.strftime("%b %d, %Y")
mo_detail["last"] = mo_detail["last"].dt.strftime("%b %d, %Y")
mo_detail.index.name = None
mo_detail = mo_detail.rename(columns={"first": "First", "last": "Last", "performances": "Perfs"})
mo_detail.style.format(
    "{:,}", subset=[c for c in mo_detail.columns if c not in ("First", "Last")]
)

And by day of week:


Code
mo_day.style.format(
    {
        "performances": "{:,}",
        "attendees": "{:,}",
        "attendees per performance": "{:.1f}",
        "share of attendees": "{:.1%}",
    }
)

Opera audiences didn’t privilege the weekend: the midweek opera nights drew the most people per performance (Thursday 76, versus 49 on Friday and 38 on Saturday, the weakest day per show — diluted, as in theater, by Saturday matinees). The sample is small (a few midweek performances, all in the 2013–2017 festival era), so treat the midweek numbers as suggestive rather than precise.
crumbs: 3  When do people come to Hubbard Hall?

title: 3  When do people come to Hubbard Hall?
text:
3.4 Which days are busiest overall?
The coarser view: every event of every kind, whether or not it was part of a run. Counts here are registrations (a proxy for headcount), and because most events are scheduled on the day the organizers chose, this mostly reflects what gets booked rather than audience preference — use the within-run sections above for that. It is kept for context.


Code
cat_order = ["theater", "music", "dance", "opera", "class"]
named = regs[regs["event_minorcat"].isin(cat_order)].assign(
    cat=lambda d: d["event_minorcat"]
)
combo = pd.concat([named, regs[regs["event_majorcat"] == "class"].assign(cat="class")])
combo = combo.dropna(subset=["weekday"])
tab = combo.groupby(["cat", "weekday"], observed=True).size().reset_index(name="attendance")
fig = px.bar(
    tab,
    x="weekday",
    y="attendance",
    facet_col="cat",
    category_orders={"cat": cat_order, "weekday": WEEKDAY_ORDER},
    color_discrete_sequence=[BLUE],
)
fig.update_layout(yaxis_title="Registrations")
fig.for_each_xaxis(lambda a: a.update(title=""))
fig.update_traces(hovertemplate="%{y:,.0f}<extra></extra>")
style(fig).show()




                            
                                            


Figure 3.4: Registrations by day of week — one panel per programming type




Theater peaks Saturday; music peaks Sunday. Classes spread across the week with a Friday peak.
How far people travel — including whether Friday theater audiences travel farther than Sunday’s — is in How far do people travel to Hubbard Hall?.
crumbs: 3  When do people come to Hubbard Hall?

title: 4  How far do people travel to Hubbard Hall?
text:
4.1 Do weekend theater audiences travel differently?
The donor-geography chapter asked where the money comes from; this one asks where the people come from. Counts are registrations by the registering household’s distance band (straight-line miles — see Methodology); registrations without a geocodable address (about 2%) are dropped.
Registrations by distance band and programming type:
Classes serve the immediate community — 76% of class registrations come from within 10 miles (dance is similar at 76%). Performances pull from a much wider region: only 54% of theater, 59% of music, and 52% of opera registrations are that local, and roughly a third of theater (30%) and opera (34%) registrations come from beyond 20 miles.
For theater only, registrations from each distance band on each day of the week — “do Friday/Saturday audiences travel farther than Sunday’s?”
Code
theater = regs[regs["event_minorcat"] == "theater"].dropna(subset=["weekday", "distance_band"])
day_band = (
    theater.groupby(["weekday", "distance_band"], observed=True)
    .size()
    .unstack("distance_band")
    .reindex(index=WEEKDAY_ORDER, columns=BANDS)
    .fillna(0)
    .astype(int)
)
far_day = (day_band[BANDS[-1]] / day_band.sum(axis=1))
t = day_band.copy()
t["beyond 20 mi"] = far_day
t.style.format("{:,}", subset=BANDS).format({"beyond 20 mi": pct})

Sunday draws the farthest-traveling theater audience: 37% of Sunday registrations come from beyond 20 miles, versus 25% on Friday and 29% on Saturday. Friday is the most local night. That fits the within-run finding in When do people come to Hubbard Hall?: the Sunday matinee is not a neighborhood habit — it is when out-of-area visitors come.
crumbs: 4  How far do people travel to Hubbard Hall?

title: 5  How do giving and participation overlap?
text:
Each household is flagged is_donor (gave a SUCCEEDED individual gift), is_patron (registered for a performance), and is_class_taker (registered for a class).


Code
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
from hh.io import read_parquet
from hh.viz import style, CATEGORICAL

hh = read_parquet("processed", "households_summary.parquet")
counts = (
    pd.Series(
        {
            "Donors": hh["is_donor"].sum(),
            "Patrons (performances)": hh["is_patron"].sum(),
            "Class-takers": hh["is_class_taker"].sum(),
            "Donor and class": (hh["is_donor"] & hh["is_class_taker"]).sum(),
            "Donor and patron": (hh["is_donor"] & hh["is_patron"]).sum(),
            "All three": (hh["is_donor"] & hh["is_patron"] & hh["is_class_taker"]).sum(),
        },
        name="households",
    )
    .reset_index()
    .rename(columns={"index": "group"})
)




Code
fig = px.bar(counts, x="group", y="households", color="group", color_discrete_sequence=CATEGORICAL)
fig.update_layout(showlegend=False, xaxis_title="", yaxis_title="Households")
fig.update_traces(hovertemplate="%{y:,.0f}<extra></extra>")
style(fig).show()




                            
                                            


Figure 5.1: Households in each giving / participation group




Which performances the donor households actually attend is in What performances do donors attend?; how far donors live from the hall is in Where do donors live?.
crumbs: 5  How do giving and participation overlap?

title: 6  What performances do donors attend?
text:
6.1 Which offerings drew the most donor attendance?
The overlap chapter showed that most donor households also attend; this one asks what they attend. Each registration is tagged with whether the registering household has ever given a SUCCEEDED individual gift (“donor household”). Performance attendance is headcount (succeeded ticket attendees — see Methodology), attributed to the registering household; canceled, moved, and rehearsal events are excluded.
Donor households account for 68% of all performance attendance. By genre:
Donor households are the majority of every genre’s audience — highest for music (77%), lowest for dance (62%), with theater at 64%. Selling tickets and cultivating donors are not separate audiences at Hubbard Hall.
Attendance from donor households by offering — production titles where the run mapping applies (recurring titles such as Winter Carnival aggregate across their annual runs), otherwise the event’s own name:
Code
perf["offering"] = perf["event_name"].map(lambda n: match_production(n) or n)
g = perf.groupby("offering").agg(
    genre=("event_minorcat", "first"),
    first_year=("starts_on", lambda s: int(s.dt.year.min())),
    attendees=("attendees", "sum"),
)
g["donor attendees"] = perf[perf["is_donor"]].groupby("offering")["attendees"].sum()
g["donor attendees"] = g["donor attendees"].fillna(0).astype(int)
g["donor share"] = g["donor attendees"] / g["attendees"]
top = g.sort_values("donor attendees", ascending=False).head(15)
top.index.name = None
top.style.format(
    {"attendees": "{:,}", "donor attendees": "{:,}", "donor share": pct}
)

Do donor and non-donor households split their activity differently? Registration mix (all event types, registrations rather than headcount):
Code
mix = (
    m.groupby([m["is_donor"].map({True: "Donor households", False: "Non-donor households"}),
               "event_majorcat"], observed=True)
    .size()
    .unstack("event_majorcat")
    .fillna(0)
)
mix = mix.div(mix.sum(axis=1), axis=0)
mix.index.name = None
mix.style.format("{:.0%}")

crumbs: 6  What performances do donors attend?

title: Appendix A — Methodology
text:
A.1 Data and reproducibility
This book is built from data pulled directly from the Neon CRM API. Every figure is reproducible: data is extracted, cleaned, geocoded, and summarized by the hh package, then read from saved parquet tables in data/20_processed/. All numbers in the prose are computed from the data at render time, so they stay correct as the data is refreshed. Every API pull is recorded in a manifest (endpoint, filters, timestamps, row counts, file hashes, code commit), and raw extracts are immutable — see the project README for the full provenance story.
All published charts and tables are aggregates (counts and dollar totals by category, distance band, and day of week) — no individual is identified.

title: Appendix A — Methodology
text:
A.2 Geography
Distances are straight-line (haversine) from Hubbard Hall (25 E Main St, Cambridge NY) to each household, bucketed into half-open mile bands. Households whose only address is a PO box are placed at their ZIP’s centroid and flagged “approximate”; they are excluded from the tight 1-mile count so it stays honest.

title: Appendix A — Methodology
text:
A.3 Attendance headcount
Registration counts understate attendance: one registration is a purchase, and averages about two attendees. Where the text says headcount, it counts ticket attendees whose registration status is SUCCEEDED — this also automatically drops canceled and refunded attendees.

title: Appendix A — Methodology
text:
A.4 Reconstructing multi-day production runs
Each performance night is a separate Neon event (e.g. “Fun Home - Friday, February 3 at 7:30 pm”) — there is no field linking the nights of a run. Runs are therefore reconstructed by a manually curated mapping from event names to productions (hh.analytics.productions):

Mapping. Every theater event name matches exactly one production title pattern (coverage is exhaustive and tested). For music and opera, only titles with multiple performances are mapped; one-off concerts cannot form a run and are dropped.
Runs. A production’s performances are split into separate runs when consecutive dates are more than 45 days apart — this separates recurring titles (Winter Carnival of New Work, Whispering Bones, Bread & Puppet) into their yearly runs.
Exclusions. Events that were not regular public performances are dropped before mapping: canceled or moved dates, test events, open/dress/pay-what-you-will rehearsals, online productions, day-long special events, and weekday 10 am school shows.
Full-weekend runs. The within-run day-of-week analysis keeps only runs that played all three of Friday, Saturday, and Sunday, so day comparisons hold the production constant.
Time slots. A performance is classified a matinee if its name contains “2 pm” or “matinee”; otherwise evening.
